mainEntity.name: How much does a Jaguar XJ 5.0 V8 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned AJ133 engine typically costs £4,000–£10,000 supply only depending on which version (naturally aspirated or supercharged), with used units available from £2,000–£4,500. Fitting adds £2,000–£3,000. Customers have reported dealer quotes of around £15,000–£30,000. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your XJ.

mainEntity.name: Is the Jaguar XJ 5.0 V8 engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The AJ133 is a powerful and refined V8, but it has well-documented weaknesses. The most common serious problems are timing chain tensioner guide wear (steel tensioner pistons eat into aluminium guides), plastic coolant pipe failure (cracks at the seams), and PCV diaphragm failure. The naturally aspirated version is slightly more reliable than the supercharged. The 2017/2018 revisions to coolant pipes are significant improvements.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Jaguar XJ 5.0 V8?

acceptedAnswer.text: Timing chain tensioner and guide wear (rattling on cold start — the most critical concern), plastic coolant pipe failure (cracking at seams — can cause catastrophic overheating), PCV diaphragm failure (oil consumption, lean running codes), carbon build-up on intake valves, and supercharger coupler wear on SC versions.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Jaguar XJ 5.0 V8 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: XJ values range from £8,000–£25,000+ depending on condition and spec. A £4,000–£10,000 reconditioned engine is 20–50% of the vehicle's value — apply the Jaguar 60% scrap threshold. If the car is a cherished example with good bodywork, replacement is often worthwhile. However, given the dealer quotes of £15,000–£30,000, ensure you understand the full cost before committing.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Jaguar XJ 5.0 V8 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The engine code is AJ133 — it's the only 5.0-litre V8 used in the XJ. You can find it on the VIN plate, in the vehicle documentation, or on a label on the engine itself. Production ran from 2009 to 2019.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the AJ133 timing chain tensioner, or does the engine need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: The timing chain tensioners and guides can be replaced — they are wear items. Replacement costs £2,000–£4,000 and is a labour-intensive job (18+ hours). If the chain fails and jumps timing, the engine is typically destroyed. A preventative tensioner and guide replacement is far cheaper than a full engine replacement (£4,000+). The early Tsubaki chains are no longer available — if they fail, all sprockets must be updated to INA 8mm chains. At the first sign of rattling from the front of the engine, book an inspection immediately.

replacementCosts.h2: Jaguar XJ 5.0 V8 Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the current XJ 5.0 V8 market. Customers have reported dealer quotes of around £15,000–£30,000 for engine replacement. Used engines typically range from £3,000 to £6,000. The supercharged version commands a significant premium over the naturally aspirated version. See the Era Map above for generation‑specific context.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an XJ 5.0 V8 engine typically takes 14–18 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer). The AJ133 is a complex, tightly-packaged V8 that requires specialist knowledge.

problems.title: Timing Chain Tensioner and Guide Wear (Both Versions)
problems.symptoms: Rattling noise on cold start (especially from the front of the engine), metallic ticking, engine warning light, cam timing errors, "engine reduced power" message, eventual chain slip
problems.repairCost: £2,000–£4,000 (timing chain, tensioners, guides and sprocket replacement)
problems.replacementCost: £4,000–£7,000 (if chain jumps and causes valve interference)

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: the timing chain tensioners are the single biggest risk on the AJ133

problems.recommendation: The most common serious engine problems on the AJ133 are loose/stretched timing chains due to the poor design of the timing chain tensioners — specifically the soft spots on the guide rails that the tensioners push against. The steel tensioner pistons ram directly into aluminium guide rails, and since steel is harder than aluminium, the tensioner pistons eat into the rails and lead to slack in the chain. JLR has acknowledged timing chain tensioner issues in the 5.0L V8, with primary TSB SSM 71932 addressing rattling noise and premature wear. The early Tsubaki 6.35mm pitch chains are no longer available — if they fail, all sprockets and hardware must be updated to accommodate INA 8mm chains. A timing job on a 5.0 V8 calls for over 18 hours of book labour. If you hear rattling on cold start, book an inspection immediately. Preventative tensioner and guide replacement is far cheaper than a full engine replacement.

failureLink.label: Read the full timing chain failure guide

problems.title: Plastic Coolant Pipe Failure (Both Versions)
problems.symptoms: Coolant loss with no visible external leak, rising temperature gauge, steam from the engine bay, overheating, bubbling coolant, warning light

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: a cracked plastic coolant pipe can dump coolant rapidly, leading to catastrophic engine failure

problems.recommendation: The most unreliable part of the AJ133 is the engine coolant system, specifically the plastic pipes, hoses and tubes — particularly the two cross-over pipes at the front of the engine and the heater pipe at the rear. These pipes are notorious for cracking at the seams (two-piece plasti-welded). Early V8s (from 2009) had much better alloy main coolant pipes, but they were switched to the crappy plastic design in 2012. Plastic cooling pipes on 2010–2015 era XJs are a major weak point, with the plastic seam being particularly vulnerable. The pipes were revised around 2017/2018 to smooth one-piece designs which are far less problematic but still not immune to failure. Check coolant levels regularly. If you see bubbling coolant, stop driving immediately — the engine may already be damaged.

failureLink.label: Read the full coolant leak guide

problems.title: PCV Diaphragm Failure and Carbon Build-up (Both Versions)
problems.symptoms: Increased oil consumption, rough idle, lean running error codes, blue smoke on startup, poor fuel economy
problems.repairCost: £300–£800 (PCV diaphragm replacement and intake valve cleaning)
problems.recommendation: Rubber PCV diaphragms rip with age and cause oil consumption and possible lean running error codes. Carbon fouling of the intake valves that requires removing the intake to clean is common — it was "really bad" on some 5.0L examples. The naturally aspirated V8 is easier to work on than the supercharged version for PCV replacement. If you notice oil consumption increasing or rough idle, have the PCV system and intake valves inspected.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: XJ 5.0 V8 — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Labour-intensive but far cheaper than a full engine replacement
If chain jumps and valves hit pistons, replacement is the only option
Replace with revised one‑piece pipes — catch before overheating
Simple replacement — restore oil consumption and idle
Viable on a low‑mileage example; borderline on high‑mileage

buyingChecks.title: Buying This XJ 5.0 V8? Check These First
buyIf: * Post‑2017/2018 model with revised one‑piece coolant pipes — the plastic seam weakness was addressed
* Evidence of timing chain tensioner and guide replacement — the single most important check on the AJ133
* Full service history with oil changes at 8,000–10,000 mile intervals (not 16,000 miles)
* Naturally aspirated version — slightly more reliable than the supercharged
* A well-maintained example where the previous owner understood the AJ133's needs
avoidIf: * Any rattling from the front of the engine on cold start — timing chain tensioner wear is likely
* 2010–2015 era with no evidence of coolant pipe work — plastic pipes will fail
* Evidence of repeated coolant top-ups without a diagnosed leak — the cooling system is failing
* Poor service history — the AJ133 is unforgiving of the 16,000-mile oil change interval
* Any "engine reduced power" message without a clear diagnosis

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The AJ133 is one of Jaguar's most powerful and refined V8s — a genuine masterpiece of engineering. But it has well-documented weaknesses: timing chain tensioner guide wear (the steel tensioner eats into the aluminium guide), plastic coolant pipes that crack at the seams, PCV diaphragm failure, and carbon build-up. The naturally aspirated version is slightly more reliable than the supercharged. The 2017/2018 revisions to the coolant pipes and timing components are significant improvements. Buy on history, maintain on schedule, and the AJ133 will reward you with one of the finest V8 experiences of any luxury saloon. Skip the history and it will punish you.

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Jaguar XJ 5.0 V8 (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: 1. Cold Start — Listen for rattles from the front of the engine (timing chain tensioner warning — the most critical check on the AJ133)
2. Service History — including which version (NA or SC) and evidence of timing chain work
3. Coolant Level — Any unexplained loss? (plastic coolant pipe warning)
4. Oil Consumption — Does it use more than 1L per 1,000 miles? (PCV or valve stem seal warning)
5. Idle Quality — Any rough idle or hesitation? (carbon build-up or injector issues)
6. Supercharger Noise (SC) — Any rattling at idle? (coupler wear warning)
7. Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved issues?
8. MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures (catalyst, O2 sensors)

engineCodes.matchNote: This table's rows match Section 1B's Era Map exactly — one engine family across two versions (naturally aspirated and supercharged).
